what_it_does: Proposes a grain-tiered extraction schema (coarse, medium, fine) for summarizing corpus chunks, reports a small local-4b validation run, and argues for a single rich extraction bake with read-time grain projection.
observations: Defines three nested schema tiers: COARSE {about, kind, touches}, MEDIUM {+summary, +entities}, FINE {+claims, +relations, +open_questions}.
Treats grain as a resolution coordinate: resolve(grain) selects a field subset and scales the prompt.
Reports a 15-chunk local-4b trial with clean accuracy, no hallucination, and sparse fine fields when relations/threads are genuinely absent.
Lists throughput estimates: fine ~1.8 chunks/s, coarse ~7/s, binary ~18/s, noting the fine estimate is from an unsaturated batch.
Corrects the bake strategy to store the full fine superset once and project down at read time; up-projection would require re-extraction.
Status is blocked on Tim's schema approval, real throughput measurement with vLLM batching, and the lead's VRAM call to load the local extraction model.
References internal project mechanisms: fork, composition, spine, recollection, PARAM-MAP commit 678e7f6, and Tim's gate g-1781962851.
standouts: The grain axis is framed as read-time down-projection of one stored rich asset, not as separate per-grain extractions.
Fine-field sparsity is accepted as correct behavior because empty-when-absent avoids fabrication and re-extraction is wasteful.
The file functions as a schema-first gate document before a long extraction run.
conventions: Uses project-specific jargon without in-file definitions (fork, composition, spine, recollection, PARAM-MAP).
Markdown section structure with status, proven-by-use evidence, and explicit blockers.
References a prior framing as superseded and a commit hash for the corrected strategy.
Presents empirical metrics (fill-rate, throughput) to justify design choices.
concerns: Throughput numbers are extrapolated from a small, unsaturated batch; full-scale measurement is pending.
The resolver mechanism needed for read-time projection may not exist yet, with a fallback to three role variants.
Terms like chunk, corpus, fork, composition, and spine are used without local definitions.
The strategy depends on decisions and resources outside this file (Tim's nod, lead VRAM, fork/recollection measurement).
notes: A schema-first proposal that validates a three-tier extraction format on a small local run and argues for baking the rich superset once with grain-scaled read-time projection.
questions: What are the exact definitions of fork, composition, and spine in this project?
How does the resolver perform read-time projection of schema subsets?
What is the full corpus size and what constitutes a chunk?
What is Tim's gate g-1781962851 and what does approval entail?
What is recollection's role versus fork's role in the extraction pipeline?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to propose and validate an extraction schema before the bake; the file does that and also records a corrected bake strategy and current blockers, so purpose and actual align.
apparent_themes: Store rich superset once, project down at read time
Throughput/cost as a first-class design axis
Separation of concerns across project roles/layers
intention_signals: To establish a reusable, resolution-variable extraction format so future relevance determinations consume a pre-baked asset instead of re-reading raw chunks, while managing generation cost through grain-scaled prompts.
novelty: Frames extraction granularity as a resolution coordinate with a single stored superset and read-time down-projection, and treats fine-field sparsity as a desirable feature rather than a defect.
contribution: A concrete three-tier schema definition, empirical fill-rate and throughput evidence from a small local-4b trial, and a design argument for one-time rich extraction with grain-dependent read-time projection.
summary_for_embedding: A markdown proposal for a grain-tiered extraction schema (coarse, medium, fine) validated on 15 local-4b chunks, arguing to bake the full fine superset once and project it down at read time by requested grain.
intention_for_embedding: To gate and justify the extraction schema before a large-scale run by proving accuracy, fill rates, and throughput, and to align the bake strategy with a resolution-variable resolver.
